coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescence sensing, information encryption and smartphone-assisted visual detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Wei
Ling, Min
Fu, Xingqin
Wang, Liangying
Xu, Shaoe
Wang, Guanglin
Liu, Renyong
Han, Chen
Li, Gang
Zhang, Jian
Liu, Zhifang
Jin, Juncheng
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high added value from a wide range of renewable biowaste sources holds utmost significance in addressing environmental and economic concerns. Herein, we present a simple and environmentally friendly method for synthesizing high-quality fluorescent nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) using orange peel. The obtained N-CQDs were employed as an efficient on-off-on fluorescent sensor for sequential ferric ions (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) detection. The linear range for the determination of Fe3+ was observed to be 2–150 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.253 μM. Additionally, within the concentration range of 30–130 μM, the recovery fluorescence exhibited a favorable linearity with the concentration of AA. The calculated LOD for this range was found to be 1.57 μM. In addition, the prepared N-CQDs have been readily applied as information encryption. Besides, the paper based N-CQDs sensor, combined with the RGB/HSV analysis software, provides a cost-effective and portable solution for on-site fluorescence detection. To our knowledge, it is the first time that the CQDs derived from orange peel have been utilized in the domains of anti-counterfeiting and smartphone-assisted portable detection. These findings highlight the significant and diverse applications of biomass-derived CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Information encryption
On-site
Portable detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative determination of Fe3+ and AA
Detection of AA in real samples
Quenching mechanisms of N-CQDs by Fe3+
Assay of Fe3+ and AA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Open Project of Key Laboratory of Biomimetic Sensor and Detecting Technology of Anhui Province (WXZR202320), the High-level Talent Project of West Anhui University (WGKQ2022085), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62304020), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22276142), the Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2108085MB51) and Research and Innovation Team Project of West Anhui University (WXZR202302).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs (insert: HRTEM of N-CQDs); (b) XRD spectrum of N-CQDs; (c) Raman spectra of N-CQDs; The high resolution XPS peaks of C 1s (d), N 1s (e) and O1s (f).

(a) UV–vis absorption of N-GQDs (insert: Tauc's plot); (b) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (PLE) of N-CQDs (Em = 440 nm); (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs measured using different excitation wavelengths; (d) The selectivity of N-CQDs to various metal ions; (e) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs to various concentrations of Fe3+; (f) Linear plot between F0/F and the concentrations of Fe3+. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+.

(a) UV–vis absorption of Fe3+ and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of N-CQDs; (b) UV–vis absorption of Fe3+, N-CQDs and N-CQDs/Fe3+; (c) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs; (d) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs/Fe3+. Ex = excitation, Em = Emission.

(a) PL spectrum of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system after adding different concentrations of AA; (b) Linear relationship between F2/F1 and AA concentration; (c) Selective testing of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system for AA; (d) Interference study with other molecular (cAA: cother molecular = 1:3). F1 and F2 are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ before and after the addition of AA.
Information encryption of N-GQDs and the corresponding photographs of handwritten under daylight (a, b, e-h) and UV light (c, d, i-l) with a wavelength of 365 nm.

(a) Schematic diagram of portable detection; (b) Fluorescence images with addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ under a 365 nm UV light; (c) The linear relationship of RGB values with Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 100 μM; (d) The linear relationship of V values with Fe3+ concentrations from 20 to 100 μM.
Illustration for the synthesis of N-CQDs from orange peel and the application in Fe3+ and AA sensing, anti-counterfeiting and smartphone sensing platform.
Detection of AA in fruit samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescence sensing, information encryption and smartphone-assisted visual detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomimetic Sensor and Detecting Technology of Anhui Province, West Anhui University, Lu'an, 237012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomimetic Sensor and Detecting Technology of Anhui Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomimetic Sensor and Detecting Technology of Anhui Province, West Anhui University, Lu’an 237012, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuits and Electronics, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuits and Electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrated Circuits and Electronics, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high added value from a wide range of renewable biowaste sources holds utmost significance in addressing environmental and economic concerns. Herein, we present a simple and environmentally friendly method for synthesizing high-quality fluorescent nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) using orange peel. The obtained N-CQDs were employed as an efficient on-off-on fluorescent sensor for sequential ferric ions (Fe3+) and ascorbic acid (AA) detection. The linear range for the determination of Fe3+ was observed to be 2–150 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.253 μM. Additionally, within the concentration range of 30–130 μM, the recovery fluorescence exhibited a favorable linearity with the concentration of AA. The calculated LOD for this range was found to be 1.57 μM. In addition, the prepared N-CQDs have been readily applied as information encryption. Besides, the paper based N-CQDs sensor, combined with the RGB/HSV analysis software, provides a cost-effective and portable solution for on-site fluorescence detection. To our knowledge, it is the first time that the CQDs derived from orange peel have been utilized in the domains of anti-counterfeiting and smartphone-assisted portable detection. These findings highlight the significant and diverse applications of biomass-derived CQDs.

Illustration for the synthesis of N-CQDs from orange peel and its application in Fe3+ and AA sensing, anti-counterfeiting and smartphone sensing platform.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Orange peels have been proved to be useful in traditional Chinese medicine, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals applications [1–3]. However, there is still a large number of orange peel being buried and burned as garbage. In order to achieve the resource utilization of waste and avoid environmental pollution, transformation of orange peels into value-added products holds significant practical importance. As an abundant biomass waste, orange peels offer the dual advantages of low cost and sustainability, coupled with high content of carbon and oxygen, making them an intriguing source of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [4,5]. Actually, due to the unique optical properties of CQDs, they have been widely applied in various fields [6–8]. Based on this, researchers have synthesized CQDs from forestry, agriculture and food wastes, such as, orange peel [9], sugarcane [10], durian [11], lignin [12], mulberry [13], poplar leaves [14], magnolia flower [15] and lotus stem [16]. Unfortunately, the low photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY, usually less than 20 %) of biomass based CQDs mentioned above limits their application. Among them, orange peel based CQDs, displayed excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission behaviors, high chemical stability, photostability, low toxicity and biocompatibility, making them a versatile material can been effectively utilized in nonlinear optical [17], fluorescence sensing [18,19], bio-imaging [20], and solar cells [8]. However, the application scope of CQDs derived from orange peels has limitations, especially in the fields of fluorescence detection, information security and portable detection. At present, only the detection of permanganate in water and Escherichia coli in milk have been reported [18,19], while the study related to the determination of common heavy metal ions Fe3+ is still absent. As an important element in various pathological and physiological process, the maladjustment of Fe3+ significantly impacts biological systems and requires highly selective and sensitive detection [21]. In addition, ascorbic acid (AA), as an essential nutrient, its deficiency may result in common cold, cardiovascular disease, mental illness, infertility, and cancer, while superfluous intake of AA may also cause uncomfortable [22]. Hence, there is indeed a high demand for the development of facile, effective and environmentally friendly fluorescent sensors for the detection of Fe3+ and AA using CQDs derived from orange peels based on green chemistry and economical strategies. In addition, given the diverse requirements for detection platforms in different fields, especially for scenarios that require on-site monitoring, the development of sensitive, low-cost, fast, and visual on-site monitoring sensors is of great significance [23]. However, the application of CQDs from biowastes and the research on visual on-site detection of metal ions remain largely unexplored areas.

Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials are special materials that utilize fluorescence effect to achieve anti-counterfeiting purposes [24,25]. In this approach, a specific pattern or message was written using a fluorescent ink composed of specially designed fluorescent molecules or quantum dots. Under daylight conditions, this information remains concealed, ensuring its confidentiality. However, when exposed to a specific wavelength of light, such as ultraviolet light, the materialʼs fluorescence is activated, revealing the encoded message [26,27]. CQDs, particularly those derived from biomass waste, possess numerous superior features including controllable photoluminescent, low cost, good solubility, facile synthesis and eco-friendly, were regarded as a viable alternative encryption material [6,12]. Furthermore, the use of renewable resources for the production of anti-counterfeiting materials aligns with the principles of green chemistry and contributes to a more sustainable manufacturing process. Therefore, utilizing biomass derived CQDs as anti-counterfeiting materials is highly desired. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no attempt to study the information encryption based on the CQDs derived from orange peels.

Fluorescence detection approaches can be divided into two main types based on their underlying mechanisms. Namely, dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE). For example, Fe3+ ions, with their empty d orbitals, can form chelates with electron-donating groups. This interaction facilitates an electronic transition from excited state of ﬂuorophores to the d orbitals of Fe3+, resulting in the photoluminescence quenching, namely DQE [28]. On the other hand, the formed complex may be a non-ﬂuorescent ground state substance, which causes non-radiative recombination, leading to a reduction in ﬂuorescence intensity, that is SQE [29,30]. Previous research demonstrated that CQDs obtained from orange peels have a low nitrogen content and exist in the form of OC–N, C3–N and graphite-like structure of N [18,19], without strong electron donating amino groups (-NH2) which can coordinate with Fe3+. We assumed that the relative low concentrations of electron donating groups is the main reason why these CQDs are not suitable for Fe3+ and AA (or other substances) assay. To address this, incorporating amine-containing molecules, such as ethylenediamine (EDA), during the CQDs preparation process not only increases the nitrogen doping level but also introduces electron-donating groups. In addition, chemical doping of N atoms into the framework of CQDs would change the atomic and electronic structure of the matrix, improving PLQY, photochemical and photophysical stability [26,31], which are the characteristics required for information encryption [32].

With this in mind, herein, we designed a facile synthetic method for synthesizing N-CQDs from the orange peels for sequential Fe3+ and AA sensing, advanced message encryption, portable and on-site Fe3+ detection (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized N-CQDs have excitation dependent emission behavior showing a PLQY of 23.9 %. The selective detection of Fe3+ is based on the fluorescence quenching (on-off) of the N-CQDs with a LOD of 0.253 μM. AA was able to restore the fluorescence quenched by Fe3+ and the ﬂuorescence recovery (off-on) has a good linear relationship with AA concentrations with a LOD of 1.57 μM. Moreover, the sensing platform constructed based on N-CQDs can be applied for Fe3+ and AA detection in real samples. The N-CQDs were successfully utilized as fluorescence inks for anti-counterfeiting. In addition, a method for on-site monitoring of Fe3+ has been developed using an RGB/HSV values analysis on the N-CQD based portable sensing platform.

Ethanol and EDA were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.,Ltd. Metal ions, including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Cr3+ are metal chloride, were obtained from Meryer Co., Ltd. Small biological molecules, glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), malic acid (MA), tartaric acid (TA), glutathione (GSH), d-fructose (Fru), Cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA) and citric acid (CA) were acquired from Aladdin in Shanghai. Quinine sulphate dihydrate was offered by Sigma in Shanghai. All reagents purchased from commercial suppliers are analytical grade and used directly without further processing. Dialysis bag with a molecular cut off of 3500 Da was procured from Shanghai chemical laboratory equipment Co., Ltd.

The XRD patterns were collected using a TD-3700 X-ray diffractometer (Tongda, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images were measured by JEM-2100 F. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement were performed on a ThermoScientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon Raman system (excitation wavelength 532 nm). The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a UH4150 (Hitachi) spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were conducted on a PE LS-55 spectrometer. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves were obtained by Horiba transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog-3).

Orange peels were washed with deionized water and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The dried orange peels were further ground into powder. 2 g of the above powder was dispersed in 30 mL ethanol solution and stirred for 48 h at room temperature. The mixture was then centrifuged to collect the upper yellow filtrate, which served as the precursor for synthesizing N-CQDs. The obtained solution 13 mL was transferred into a Teﬂon stainless steel autoclave (30 mL), and 2 mL EDA was added. After thorough dispersion, the autoclave was placed in an oven and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was subjected to dialysis using a dialysis bag (3500 Da) in order to purify the obtained N-CQDs.

The quantum yields (QY) of N-CQDs was calculated with relative method by taking Quinine sulphate (QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the standard [19], the formula for calculation is as follows:
∅ =
∅ R
×
A R
I R
×
I A
×
n 2
n R 2
Where
∅
, A, n and I represent the quantum yields, absorbance, refractive index (1.33 for water) and integrated emission intensity, respectively. The subscript “R” refers to the referenced fluorescence dyes of Quinine sulphate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Fe3+: Fe3+ with different concentrations were respectively mixed with 1 mL of N-CQDs (0.5 mg mL−1), in order to make the reaction complete, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min before optical testing. To demonstrate the high selectivity of N-CQDs, various metal ions (100 μM), including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+, CO3 2−, NO3 − and SO4 2− were added to the N-CQDs solution, respectively. After incubating for 5 min, fluorescence changes were detected using fluorescence spectroscopy. In the interference detection experiment, the Fe3+concentration is 100 μM, and the concentration of the interfering substance is 300 μM. Moreover, for AA determination, 1 mL of N-CQDs (0.5 mg mL−1) was mixed with 1 mL of 400 μM Fe3+ solution and allowed to react for 5 min, different concentrations of AA were added to the above mixture to react for an additional 5 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectroscopy of the resulting N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA mixture was conducted. To verify the selective detection of AA by N-CQDs/Fe3+ system, 200 μM of glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), malic acid (MA), tartaric acid (TA), glutathione (GSH), d-fructose (Fru), Cysteine (Cys) and citric acid (CA) were added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The extraction of AA from fruit samples was based on previous literature [33]. Specifically, 50 g of apple, tomato, pear, grape and citrus were weighed, and the fruits were further crushed and ground. In order to ensure the stable existence of AA, oxalic acid solution (50 mL, 0.02 g L−1) was added to the fruit samples, after sufficient stirring, the samples containing AA were obtained by centrifugation. The test results were validated using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy in order to demonstrate the effectiveness of this method. The precision of the measurements was quantified using % relative standard deviations (% RSD), which can be calculated as follows:
% RSD =
Standard
Deviation
Mean
× 100

In the anti-counterfeiting process, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink (3 mg mL−1) was obtained by dissolving N-CQDs powder in deionized water without further treatment, filter papers were served as the supporting substrates. The photographs were recorded by iPhone Xs Max under daylight and UV light (365 nm) after drying at room temperature, respectively.

In the experimental procedure, filter papers with a diameter of 0.6 cm were submerged in the N-CQDs solution to fabricate paper-based sensors. After air-drying at room temperature, a specific concentration of Fe3+ was dropped onto the prepared paper. Subsequently, fluorescence images were captured under 365 nm UV light excitation, and the RGB (red-green-blue) or HSV (hue-saturation-lightness) values were directly analyzed using a smartphone app. The RGB or HSV values were calculated according to the following equation: RGB values = Red + Green + Blue [34].

In order to gain a better understanding of the structure and morphology of the obtained N-CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed for microstate characterization, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The results indicated that the N-CQDs exhibit excellent dispersion and uniform size distribution. Statistical analysis further demonstrated that the diameters of the N-CQDs are primarily in the range of 2–5 nm, with an average size of 3.72 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. S1). Additionally, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging revealed the highly crystalline structure of the N-CQDs, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1a. The measured crystal plane spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of N-CQDs [35]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of N-CQDs show a (002) peak centered at around 24° (Fig. 1b), suggesting the graphene-like structures [36]. In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c), two prominent bands are observed, the D band at 1375 cm−1, attributed to disorder or defects, and the G band at 1587 cm−1, indicative of sp2 carbon networks in the graphitized structure. The intensity ratio between the G and D bands is measured to be IG/ID = 1.2, indicating a high degree of graphitization in the N-CQDs [37]. To elucidate the functional groups present on the synthesized N-CQDs, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed. Analysis of the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2) revealed the presence of O–H/N–H (3417 cm−1) on the surface of N-GQDs [32], confirming their good solubility in water. Moreover, the stretching vibration at 2925 cm−1 and bending vibration at 1456 cm−1 corresponded to C–H [38], 1626 and 1597 cm−1 presented the CO and CC chemical bonds [20,32], respectively. The C–N vibrations observed at 1350 and 1383 cm−1 can be attributed to both the N-doping and the presence of poly-aromatic structures on the surface of the N-CQDs [39]. The peak at 1227 and 1095 cm−1 were assigned to the C–O–C and C–OH groups [38,40], respectively. These results indicated the N-CQDs were co-functionalized by strong electron-donating groups, such as –OH and –NH2, and that a large number of oxygen-containing groups were present on the surface states. This further confirmed by the XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. S3, the XPS spectrum of the N-CQDs exhibits three characteristic peaks: C1s (285.0 eV), N1s (398.0 eV), and O1s (532.0 eV), and the element proportions of N-CQDs are 66.1 % (C), 20 % (O), and 13.9 % (N), respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) presents distinct signals at 284.5 eV for CC/C–C, 285.6 eV for N–C/C–O, 286.5 eV for NC and 287.8 eV for CO [6,13,32]. The fitted N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) was deconvoluted into three components, which corresponded to N in the form of pyridinic N (398.6 eV), pyrrolic N (399.4 eV), and N–H (400.1 eV) [13]. The XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 1f) exhibited two distinguishable peaks at 531.5 and 532.7 eV, which were attributed to CO and C–O groups [41], respectively. These findings suggested the presence of N, and O-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. The formation of these functional groups during the synthesis process suggests that N-CQDs have potential applications in various fields such as sensing and information encryption, due to their unique surface chemistry.

Furthermore, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the optical properties of N-GQDs. The absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 a) exhibits two distinct excitonic absorption bands, prominently centered at approximately 276, and 380 nm, which can be assigned to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [42]. Additionally, the optical band gap of N-CQDs was determined using Tauc plot. Tauc plot is described by the equation [43]:
(
α h ν
)
1 / n
= C ×
(
h ν −
E g
)
, where α, h, and C represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant and proportionality constant, respectively. ν denotes the frequency of light and Eg is the band gap of measured materials. It is worth noting that n takes a value of 1/2 or 2 for direct or indirect band gap material. According to the fitting results (inset of Fig. 2a), the band gap of N-CQDs was calculated to be 3.18 eV. PLE spectrum of N-CQDs exhibits two peaks at 241 and 342 nm (Fig. 2b), indicating that the observed fluorescent emission can be attributed to the core state and the nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups of the N-CQDs. The fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 2c) exhibits variations corresponding to different excitation wavelengths, with the strongest fluorescence peak observed at 440 nm when excited at 340 nm. This wavelength-dependent luminescence phenomenon can be attributed to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. It is worth noting that the fluorescence peaks of N-CQDs display pronounced variations in the excitation wavelength range of 300–340 nm, which can be attributed to the different fluorescence emission groups that can be excited. In addition, the relative PLQY of N-CQDs is 23.9 % (Figs. S4 and S5, Table S1), which is higher or comparable to CQDs derived from biomass sources (Table S2). In addition, the product yield of N-CQDs was calculated to be 4.5 %. Furthermore, we tested the luminescence stability of N-CQDs, as shown in Figs. S6–S7, the results showed that the synthesized N-CQDs had excellent photochemical stability, which is a highly desirable characteristic for their practical applications. It ensures that the luminescent properties of N-CQDs remain consistent and reliable over time, even under various environmental conditions or extended exposure to light. In addition, we have conducted an investigation into the impact of solution pH on fluorescence intensity, as depicted in Fig. S8. The results illustrate that the intensity of PL displays strongest fluorescence under alkaline media. Conversely, the N-CQDs experience a notable reduction in fluorescence in acidic environments. These can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation reactions of the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs in response to varying pH values. The alteration in surface charge consequently impacts the electronic configuration of the N-CQDs, which in turn influences their fluorescent characteristics.

Based on the comprehensive analysis and discussion, we concluded that the surface of the synthesized N-CQDs have a significant abundance of functional groups on their surface. Furthermore, the N-CQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence stability. These attributes endow the N-CQDs with the potential for high-sensitivity and accurate ion detection. As shown in Fig. 2d, the commonly present ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, Co2+, CO3 2−, NO3 − and SO4 2− were selected to evaluate the selectivity of the N-CQDs based sensor. The results demonstrated that a noticeable quenching of fluorescence occurred only upon the addition of Fe3+, indicating a highly selective response of the N-CQDs to Fe3+. In addition, anti-interference tests, as shown in Fig. S8, confirmed that the presence of other substances did not interfere with the detection of Fe3+. Thus, to further investigate the influence of Fe3+, the photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs at different Fe3+ concentrations were measured, as shown in Fig. 2e. An increase in Fe3+ concentration corresponded with a significant reduction in the fluorescent intensity of the N-CQDs. Importantly, the experimental data was plotted and fitted using a linear regression analysis (Fig. 2f), the fitted equation F0/F = 0.01095 CFe 3+ + 0.9362 (R2 = 0.9932) provides an excellent fit to the experimental data in the range of 2–150 μM. We utilized the 3σ/K method to calculate the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+, where σ represents the standard deviation determined from 10 blank samples, and K corresponds to the linear correlation slope. Based on this calculation, the LOD for Fe3+ was found to be 0.253 μM, which is comparable to other literatures (Table S3). In addition, the stability and repeatability of the sensor were evaluated through both the intra- and inter-day RSD measurements. The intra-day RSD was determined by conducting three separate analyses of the analytes within the same day, while the inter-day RSD was assessed by performing three analyses on three consecutive days. At a Fe3+ concentration of 60 μM, the intra-day RSD and inter-day RSD were found to be within the ranges of 1.7 %–2.9 % and 2.1 %–3.7 %, respectively. These results demonstrate that the developed sensor exhibits excellent stability and repeatability.

As seen from Fig. S8, increased acidity results in diminished fluorescence, this phenomenon could be attributed to the protonation of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. However, as the pH of the solution gradually elevated, the fluorescence increased and eventually tended to a constant value at pH = 11. This is because the higher the pH value, the stronger the deprotonation effect, and the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs are negatively charged, resulting in electrostatic repulsion and enhanced fluorescence. However, due to the formation of precipitate Fe(OH)3 by Fe3+ in alkaline solutions, pH = 7 was chosen during the experiment. The concentration of fluorescent probes plays a pivotal role in detection experiments. An excessively high concentration can lead to self-absorption, which diminishes sensitivity. However, a concentration that is low can result in a restricted linear range, hindering the effective analysis of the target substance. Fig. S9 illustrates the variation in fluorescence of N-CQDs at varying concentrations in the presence of Fe3+ ions. As the concentration is incremented from 0.1 to 0.5 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity and sensitivity elevated. However, a further increase in concentration leads to a reduction in fluorescence and a corresponding decrease in sensitivity. Consequently, an optimal probe concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 was selected for the experiment to balance sensitivity and analytical range. Furthermore, informed by the test outcomes (Fig. S10), an incubation period of 5 min was selected for subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms of N-CQDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanisms underlying the quenching of fluorescence were analyzed in detail. As seen in Fig. 3 a, there is a substantial overlap between the maximum excitation spectra of N-CQDs and the UV–vis absorption of Fe3+, suggesting an inner filter effect (IFE) for fluorescence quenching. The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs, Fe3+, and the N-CQDs/Fe3+ mixture were measured, as shown in Fig. 3b. Changes in the N-CQDsʼ absorption spectra upon the addition of Fe3+ suggest that the observed quenching is due to static quenching. Further, to distinguish between dynamic quenching and static quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed, which is given as follows: F0/F = 1 + Ksv × [C]. In this equation, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the absence of Fe3+, while F represents the fluorescence intensity in their presence. [C] denotes the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and Ksv represents the quenching constant. Based on the above equation, the Ksv value was found to be 1.095 × 104 L mol−1 (Fig. 2f), large than the numerical range (102–103 L mol−1) for dynamic quenching process (DQE) [44], indicating that the fluorescence of N-CQDs triggered by Fe3+ is a static quenching process (SQE). Furthermore, the molecular-quenching rate constant Kq was introduced to verify that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs originated from SQE. The Kq value was calculated based on the following equation: Ksv = Kq × τ0, where τ0 represents the average fluorescent lifetime of N-CQDs without the addition of Fe3+. When Ksv = 1.095 × 104 L mol−1, τ0 = 5.56 ns. Kq was calculated to be 1.97 × 1012 M−1 s−1, much higher than the threshold value for DQE (1 × 1010 M−1 s−1) [34], providing compelling evidence that the Fe3+ triggered fluorescence quenching was attributable to SQE. Furthermore, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was used to illustrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the lifetime was fitted by a bi-exponential function, and the average lifetime τave can be determined by the following equation [45]:
τ
a v e
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
Where τ1 and τ2 represent the lifetimes of different emission groups, A1 and A2 are the corresponding weighting parameters. Importantly, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs almost unchanged even after the addition of Fe3+ ions. This observation further strengthens the conclusion that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by Fe3+ is indeed a static quenching process.

It is known that Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ in the presence of AA. The coordination ability of Fe2+ is significantly lower than that of Fe3+, resulting in the release of N-CQDs from the N-CQDs/Fe3+ complexes and a subsequent restoration of fluorescence [46,47]. Therefore, the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system can serve as a fluorescent sensing platform for AA detection according to the recovery of fluorescence. Fig. 4 a shows that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system gradually recovered after adding AA. Notably, a significant linear relationship between the F2/F1 and the AA concentration (ranging from 30 to 130 μM) is observed (Fig. 4b), where F1, F2 represent the PL intensity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ and N-CQDs/Fe3+ in the presence of AA, respectively. This relationship can be accurately curve-fitted into F2/F1 = 0.003 CAA + 1.174 (R2 = 0.9948). The LOD for AA was determined to be 1.57 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. These results are comparable with or even superior to those reported in previous studies, as shown in Table S4. To confirm the selectivity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system towards the detection of AA, several representative substances including Glu, Gly, Met, MA, TA, GSH, Fru, Cys, and CA were introduced to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system. As depicted in Fig. 4c, among these selected substrates, fluorescence was only restored when AA was added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform, indicating excellent selectivity of the platform towards AA. Moreover, the anti-interference ability of the system was also evaluated and the results are presented in Fig. 4d. It was observed that the selected interfering substances did not cause any noticeable effect on the PL intensity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform, implying robust selectivity and anti-interference properties of the proposed sensor. In addition, UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ in the presence and absence of AA were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. S12. The results illustrates a pronounced alteration in the UV–vis absorption spectra upon the introduction of AA to N-CQDs/Fe3+. Notably, the new spectrum closely resembles that of N-CQDs (Fig. 2a). This suggests that the introduction of AA facilitates the release of the N-CQDs, suppresses static quenching, and restores fluorescence. These observations are corroborated by the phenomena observed in our experimental data, as illustrated in Fig. 4a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Fe3+ and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical application of the synthesized quantum dots, CQDs were employed as fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ in water samples using standard addition methods during the experiment. Prior to analysis, varying concentrations of Fe3+ were incorporated into the water sample, and the results are presented in Table S5. The recovery range of the detection platform is 98.4–103.2 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 3.2 %, which indicates its robust application potential. In addition, the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform make it a promising solution for detecting AA in complex food samples. With its ability to accurately and reliably measure AA levels without being influenced by other substances commonly found in food, this sensor offers significant potential for applications in food quality control. To validate the applicability of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ sensing platform in detecting AA in fruit samples (such as pear, tomato, citrus, grape, and apple), the concentration of AA was estimated by the standard curve method and the results were summarized in Table 1 . To verify the accuracy of the results, the samples were also measured using UV–vis spectroscopy. Analysis of the test results demonstrated that while the methods employed were different, the results were consistent with each other, further affirming the effectiveness of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ based detection platform.

Combining the excellent luminescence efficiency, strong photochemical stability, and good solubility of N-CQDs, the performance as anti-counterfeiting materials was studied. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , to demonstrate the effectiveness of this ink, we tested it by written the number “2023” (Fig. 5a), the English characters “N-CQDs” (Fig. 5b), and the Chinese name of West Anhui University (Fig. 5e–h) on commercially available filter paper. Under normal daylight conditions, these contents are barely visible. However, when illuminated with a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm, they emit a bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 5c and d, Fig. 5i–l). This effect serves to effectively safeguard against counterfeiting by providing a secure, visually distinctive means of information encryption. Therefore, the successful application of N-CQDs make them an effective and practical tool for enhancing the security of important documents and valuable products, offering a reliable means of protecting against fraud and unauthorized replication.

The detection of analytes in a portable manner has been hindered by the complexity of operations and the lack of popularity of laboratory instruments. Fluorescence sensors based on smartphones offer advantages such as portability, flexibility, cost-effectiveness, user-friendliness, and real-time detection capabilities. These advantages make smartphones a powerful analysis tool applicable in various fields. Based on this, we have developed a convenient paper-based N-CQD sensor that enables rapid sample detection with the assistance of a smartphone platform. The smartphone-assisted visual detection strategy is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. In Fig. 6b, fluorescent photos of N-CQDs with varying Fe3+ concentrations were captured using the smartphoneʼs camera. A color picker and capture application was then utilized to automatically extract the fluorescence image features and convert them into machine-readable information, specifically RGB or HSV values. The RGB values serve as a parameter for quantifying Fe3+. Fig. 6c demonstrates that as the Fe3+ concentration increases, the RGB values decrease. A linear relationship between RGB values and Fe3+ concentration was observed within the range of 0–100 μM (R2 = 0.9971), with the equation y = −1.1698 x + 414.233. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.016 μM (3σ/slope), lower than the maximal residue limit of 5.36 μM for drinking water set by the WHO (World Health Organization) and EPA (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6d, there was a linear correlation between V value and Fe3+ within the concentration range of 20–100 μM, with the equation y = −0.28 x + 106.8 (R2 = 0.9816). Therefore, the designed paper-based N-CQD sensor, coupled with smartphone technology, offers a practical and efficient solution for portable, on-site quantitative monitoring of Fe3+.

In summary, our study introduces the utilization of widely available orange peel as a precursor for synthesizing N-CQDs with remarkable properties. The resulting N-CQDs exhibit a PLQY of 23.9 % and possess uniform particle sizes, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent optical characteristics, thus making it available to monitor Fe3+ and AA with selectivity and sensitivity through a fluorescence on-off-on response, with LOD of 0.253 and 1.57 μM, respectively. Notably, the platform constructed using the N-CQDs can be employed for Fe3+ and AA detection in real samples. Furthermore, we explored the potential of N-CQDs as a promising material for encryption of secrecy information. Additionally, the portable smartphone platform based on the paper-based N-CQD sensor provides a reliable and convenient approach for rapid and accurate on-site detection of Fe3+. As a result, the fluorescent N-CQDs derived from orange peels present a viable alternative platform for chemosensors, anti-counterfeiting, and hold great promise in the field of portable detection. Meanwhile, we anticipate that the orange peels can be used in sensing devices, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis.

Wei Fu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Min Ling: Visualization, Investigation. Xingqin Fu: Validation, Methodology. Liangying Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Shaoe Xu: Validation, Investigation. Guanglin Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Renyong Liu: Resources, Data curation. Chen Han: Investigation, Data curation. Gang Li: Resources, Data curation. Jian Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Zhifang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration. Juncheng Jin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Open Project of Key Laboratory of Biomimetic Sensor and Detecting Technology of Anhui Province (WXZR202320), the High-level Talent Project of West Anhui University (WGKQ2022085), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62304020), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22276142), the Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2108085MB51) and Research and Innovation Team Project of West Anhui University (WXZR202302).

Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fu W, Ma J, Qiao Z, Xu L, Wang L, Ling M, et al. Ultrasound-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent sulfur quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in real samples. Langmuir 2023;39(46):16349-16357.
